FBL56.potentialContendersIntro: Germany took bronze at Paris 2024. The United States and Brazil, the Paris finalists, are also expected to reach the semifinals and could appear here if they lose.

FBL57.blurb:
The men's gold medal match at the Rose Bowl, the tournament's final act and the biggest prize in international football outside the World Cup. Spain won gold at Paris 2024, defeating France in the final, and both nations are projected to contend again if they qualify.

This is the match two weeks of group and knockout football have built toward—90 minutes (or more) to decide the champion. The Rose Bowl's 92,000-seat bowl transforms into a global stage, with the entire soccer world watching.

Pasadena's historic amphitheater has hosted World Cup finals, college football championships, and five Super Bowls. The evening kickoff and Southern California summer weather provide ideal conditions, and the concrete colosseum's acoustics amplify every chant, every roar, every moment of a match that could define careers.
FBL57.potentialContendersIntro: Spain defeated France in the Paris 2024 final. Both nations are expected to reach the knockout stage and could meet again for gold.

FBL58.blurb:
The women's gold medal match crowns the tournament champion at the Rose Bowl, the final session of the 2028 football competition. The United States won at Paris 2024, defeating Brazil in a rematch that lived up to its billing, and both nations are expected to contend again.

This is the pinnacle—two teams, 90 minutes, one gold medal. The winner leaves as champion; the loser settles for silver after two weeks of tournament football. The Rose Bowl's 89,000 capacity and midday kickoff guarantee a sun-drenched, sold-out atmosphere.

Pasadena's historic bowl has hosted the biggest matches in American soccer history, and this one joins that list. The concrete amphitheater's elliptical design wraps the pitch in steep banks of seats, delivering sightlines and acoustics that turn a championship match into a defining moment.
FBL58.potentialContendersIntro: The United States defeated Brazil in the Paris 2024 final. Both teams are projected to reach the knockout stage and could meet again for gold.

FEN01.blurb:
The fencing competition opens with parallel elimination rounds for women's épée and men's sabre. Over six hours, 64 fencers in each weapon duel through tables of 32 and 16 down to the final eight, fighting for spots in evening medal bouts.

Épée scores with any touch on the opponent's entire body; sabre allows cuts and thrusts above the waist. Prelims move fast—three-minute bouts to 15 points—with minimal breaks between pistes.

Downtown LA Convention Center's Hall 1 seats roughly 5,000 under soaring 36-foot ceilings. The flexible exhibit space transforms into a fencing arena with multiple pistes running simultaneously, letting you watch several eliminations unfold at once.
FEN01.potentialContendersIntro: Italy's women's épée team and South Korea's men's sabre squad claimed Paris 2024 team golds; athletes from those rosters could appear if they qualify individually.

FEN02.blurb:
Two individual gold medals get decided in one electric evening session. Women's épée and men's sabre semifinals narrow the field to final pairs, then bronze and gold bouts crown champions.

Épée is chess with swords—defensive, tactical, often decided by centimeters. Sabre explodes with slashing attacks and lightning exchanges. Medal bouts are first to 15, three periods, no do-overs.

Prime-time finals under Convention Center lights draw roars from the crowd. The piste strips gleam, electronic scoring lights flash, and medalists celebrate where the 1984 Games press corps once filed stories.
FEN02.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 team medalists from Italy, South Korea, Hungary, and France could field strong individual contenders if they qualify for 2028.

FEN03.blurb:
Day two brings men's épée and women's foil to the 14-meter piste. Another marathon elimination session whittles 128 starters down to quarterfinalists across both weapons.

Foil targets the torso only, rewarding precision over épée's full-body chaos. Men's épée favors patience and timing—bouts can hinge on a single counterattack. Expect tactical probing and sudden explosive touches.

Seven-plus hours of nonstop fencing means catching early-round upsets, rising talents, and veterans grinding through grueling tables. Multiple pistes run concurrently in the repurposed downtown exhibit hall.
FEN03.potentialContendersIntro: France's épée strength and Poland's Paris 2024 bronze suggest European depth; Japan and Italy have foil pedigree from recent team results.

FEN04.blurb:
Men's épée and women's foil crown individual champions in back-to-back medal rounds. Four semifinals set up two gold-medal showdowns, with bronze bouts offering consolation glory.

Foil's torso-only target area demands surgical accuracy; épée's whole-body rules create tense standoffs. Both weapons race to 15 points across three three-minute periods, and tie scores trigger sudden-death overtime.

Evening finals pack the Convention Center with fans who know the sport's nuances. The electronic scoring buzzer, the clang of blades, and the referee's calls in French create a layered soundscape unique to fencing.
FEN04.potentialContendersIntro: France, Poland, Italy, and Japan produced Paris 2024 team medalists in these weapons; their individual stars could contend if qualified for 2028.

FEN05.blurb:
Women's sabre and men's foil complete the individual prelim slate. Sabre's slashing aggression contrasts with foil's point-work finesse as 64 fencers per weapon fight through elimination tables.

Sabre above-the-waist rules reward explosive footwork and hand speed. Foil punishes wild attacks—only clean touches to the torso count. Both demand stamina; fencers can face five or six bouts in one session.

The Convention Center's high ceilings and multiple pistes make this a choose-your-own-adventure viewing experience. Wander between strips to catch different styles, or camp at one to follow a contender's journey.
FEN05.potentialContendersIntro: South Korea, France, Italy, and Japan have medaled recently in sabre and foil team events; their individual pipelines could produce 2028 medalists.

FEN06.blurb:
The last individual golds are won in women's sabre and men's foil finals. Semifinals cull the field, then bronze and gold bouts deliver four more medalists to close individual competition.

Men's foil has seen Japanese dominance and Italian tradition clash in recent years. Women's sabre is wide open, with multiple nations fielding world-class talent. Expect high-velocity action and photo-finish touches.

Prime-time atmosphere in downtown LA, with medal ceremonies following each weapon's finals. The crowd's energy peaks as champions salute and national anthems echo through the hall.
FEN06.potentialContendersIntro: Japan's men's foil team gold at Paris 2024 and South Korea's sabre strength suggest potential individual stars if they advance to 2028.

FEN07.blurb:
Women's épée shifts to team format: three fencers per squad, nine relay-style bouts to 45 total points. Eight qualified nations duel through quarterfinals and semifinals, with losers moving to classification rounds.

Team épée amplifies individual tension—each fencer inherits the score from her predecessor, building or erasing leads. Italy, France, and Poland swept Paris 2024 medals and could return with seasoned rosters.

Afternoon session means accessible tickets for a format that rewards strategy and depth. The Convention Center's theater-style setup offers clear sightlines to watch team tactics unfold across relay bouts.
FEN07.potentialContendersIntro: Italy won gold, France took silver, and Poland claimed bronze in women's épée team at Paris 2024; all three could field competitive squads again.

FEN08.blurb:
Two team épée medals are decided: bronze bout at 5:30 PM, gold final at 6:40 PM. Italy, France, and Poland stand atop the Paris 2024 podium and could reprise that rivalry if rosters hold.

Team finals to 45 points play out over nine mini-matches, each fencer rotating in. Momentum swings fast—a strong middle bout can erase a double-digit deficit. The gold match crowns the world's best épée nation.

Evening finals draw passionate national fan sections. Expect chants, flags, and eruptions when a fencer lands the clinching touch. The Convention Center's exhibit-hall acoustics amplify every roar.
FEN08.potentialContendersIntro: Italy, France, and Poland swept Paris 2024 women's épée team medals and remain projected podium threats for 2028.

FEN09.blurb:
Men's sabre team: South Korea, Hungary, and France topped Paris 2024 and could renew their rivalry. Eight nations compete in table-of-8 quarters and semifinals, with bronze bout concluding the afternoon.

Sabre's slashing tempo makes team events a spectacle—bouts erupt in flurries, touches pile up fast, and leads evaporate. South Korea's explosive style clashes with Hungary's technical mastery and France's versatility.

Morning-to-afternoon session offers budget-friendly entry to team competition. Watch quarterfinal upsets or track medal favorites through elimination rounds on multiple pistes in the downtown hall.
FEN09.potentialContendersIntro: South Korea won gold, Hungary took silver, and France claimed bronze in men's sabre team at Paris 2024; all could return as favorites.

FEN10.blurb:
Men's sabre team bronze and gold finals decide podium placement. South Korea's Paris 2024 gold puts a target on their backs; Hungary and France both have deep sabre traditions.

Team sabre races to 45 points through nine relay bouts, three fencers per side. Explosive exchanges and rapid scoring make for edge-of-seat drama. The gold match often features multi-Games veterans and rising stars side by side.

Evening session, prime viewing for the weapon's most athletic displays. The Convention Center crowd feeds off sabre's kinetic energy—every slashing attack draws gasps, every parry-riposte earns applause.
FEN10.potentialContendersIntro: South Korea, Hungary, and France swept Paris 2024 men's sabre team medals and are likely to contend again in 2028.

FEN11.blurb:
Men's épée team table of 8 through semifinals unfold over seven hours. Hungary, Japan, and Czechia swept Paris 2024; all could return with experienced squads.

Team épée to 45 points rewards tactical depth—each fencer inherits the running score and tries to extend the lead or claw back. Nine relay bouts test endurance and nerves as much as technique.

Long daytime session offers a marathon viewing experience. Camp at one piste or roam the Convention Center hall to catch quarterfinal drama, semifinal tension, and classification bouts for final team standings.
FEN11.potentialContendersIntro: Hungary won gold, Japan took silver, and Czechia claimed bronze in men's épée team at Paris 2024; all remain projected medal threats.

FEN12.blurb:
Men's épée team bronze and gold finals close the épée program. Hungary's Paris 2024 gold, Japan's silver, and Czechia's bronze set the stage for a potential rematch.

Nine relay bouts to 45 points test every fencer's stamina and composure. Épée's whole-body target allows sudden momentum swings—one counterattack can shift a match. The gold final often goes down to the wire.

Evening finals atmosphere in downtown LA, with national pride on the line. Medal ceremonies follow, capping four days of épée competition under the Convention Center's soaring ceilings.
FEN12.potentialContendersIntro: Hungary, Japan, and Czechia swept Paris 2024 men's épée team medals and could dominate the 2028 podium again.

FEN13.blurb:
Women's foil team quarterfinals and semifinals determine medal-round matchups. The United States won gold at Paris 2024, with Italy and Japan rounding out the podium.

Foil's torso-only target demands precision across nine relay bouts to 45 points. Team chemistry matters—veteran anchors often close tight matches, while rising fencers bring fresh legs to middle rounds.

Afternoon session, accessible pricing for a format showcasing national depth. The Convention Center's multi-piste setup lets you follow favorites through elimination rounds or discover underdogs making semifinal pushes.
FEN13.potentialContendersIntro: The United States won gold, Italy took silver, and Japan claimed bronze in women's foil team at Paris 2024; all could return strong.

FEN14.blurb:
Women's foil team bronze and gold finals award the last foil medals. The U.S., Italy, and Japan swept Paris 2024 and could reprise that podium battle.

Nine relay bouts to 45, three fencers per nation, strategy layered on athleticism. The gold match often features multi-Games veterans and young phenoms side by side, national pride fueling every touch.

Evening finals energy in downtown LA, with medal ceremonies capping the women's foil program. Expect raucous national cheering sections and emotional celebrations on the piste.
FEN14.potentialContendersIntro: The United States, Italy, and Japan swept Paris 2024 women's foil team medals and are projected to battle for podium spots again.

FEN15.blurb:
Men's foil team closes fencing competition with table-of-8 quarters and semifinals. Japan's Paris 2024 gold, Italy's silver, and France's bronze suggest a European-Asian showdown could repeat.

Team foil to 45 points rewards tactical variety—different fencers exploit different styles, and coaches swap lineups to counter opponents. Nine relay bouts build drama as leads change hands.

Final morning session of the Games fencing schedule. Catch quarterfinal upsets or semifinal battles in the Convention Center hall before evening medal finals decide the last team champion.
FEN15.potentialContendersIntro: Japan won gold, Italy took silver, and France claimed bronze in men's foil team at Paris 2024; all could contend for podium spots in 2028.

FEN16.blurb:
Two bouts decide the medals in men's foil team fencing. Japan defends the gold they claimed in Paris, where Italy took silver and France earned bronze on home turf.

Each team fields three fencers who compete in a relay format across nine total matchups, with scores carrying forward—momentum can swing wildly as blades flash and the clock runs. Squads strategize their lineup order to exploit matchups and preserve their closers for decisive bouts.

The action unfolds on raised pistes under bright lights in downtown LA's Convention Center, where the rapid exchanges and electronic scoring boards make every touch visible. The crowd sits close enough to hear blades clash and fencers' tactical calls between bouts.
FEN16.potentialContendersIntro: Japan claimed gold in Paris 2024, with Italy taking silver and France bronze. If those nations return their core squads, expect elite-level team fencing.

potentialContenders.name: United States
potentialContenders.note: Potential contender with home crowd advantage

FEN17.blurb:
A full day traces the path from quarterfinals to the medal-round doorstep in women's team sabre. Eight squads enter; two will advance to the evening gold-medal bout, two more to the bronze match.

Team sabre is the fastest weapon discipline—attacks are explosive, parries instantaneous, and bouts can hinge on a single flurry. Ukraine, South Korea, and Japan medaled in Paris; their projected 2028 rosters could reshape the bracket if key veterans return or new talents emerge.

The table of 8 begins mid-morning, semifinals follow in early afternoon, with classifications wrapping by late afternoon. LA Convention Center's Hall 1 seats spectators theater-style around multiple pistes, letting you track parallel bouts and catch every pivotal touch on the big screens.
FEN17.potentialContendersIntro: Ukraine won gold in Paris 2024, with South Korea silver and Japan bronze. Those nations are projected to field strong squads again, though rosters remain fluid.

FEN18.blurb:
The final night of team fencing awards two more medal sets in women's sabre. Ukraine enters as defending champions from Paris, where South Korea claimed silver and Japan bronze.

Bronze is decided first—a single bout between semifinal losers, winner takes third place. Then the gold-medal match: best-of-45 touches across nine fencer pairings, with tactical substitutions and score carryovers creating a chess match at lightning speed.

Prime-time slot, medals on the line, and the downtown Convention Center crowd leaning in close. Sabre's attacking style guarantees action—long rallies are rare, decisive moments arrive in bursts, and the electronic scoring lights flash constantly as fencers lunge and retreat along the piste.
FEN18.potentialContendersIntro: Ukraine won gold in Paris 2024, with South Korea taking silver and Japan bronze. If those core squads return, the podium could feature familiar faces.

potentialContenders.name: United States
potentialContenders.note: Home nation with rising sabre program

FFB01.blurb:
Flag football makes its Summer Games debut with the opening session of men's and women's preliminaries at Exposition Park Stadium. Six games across both tournaments kick off this historic addition to the program, showcasing the sport's explosive, non-contact format on a compact 70-yard field.

Each preliminary features 5-on-5 action with 20-minute halves, setting the stage for medal contention. Mexico arrives as the top-ranked women's team per IFAF standings, while the USA men are projected to dominate their pool.

The open-air stadium adjacent to the historic LA Memorial Coliseum brings downtown skyline views and intense LA energy. Get in early to witness history as flag football joins the world stage for the first time.
FFB01.potentialContendersIntro: The women's field is led by top-ranked Mexico, with Great Britain, Japan, and Canada expected to qualify. USA men are projected as favorites based on international form.

potentialContenders.name: Mexico
potentialContenders.note: Ranked No. 1 in IFAF women's flag football standings

potentialContenders.note: Expected to qualify for women's tournament

FFB02.blurb:
Saturday evening brings the second preliminary session under the lights at Exposition Park Stadium. Three men's and three women's games continue the opening day of competition as teams battle for semifinal positioning in the 6-team tournaments.

Expect fast-paced scoring and aggressive flag pulls as squads refine their strategies. The compact field and 20-minute halves emphasize quick decision-making and explosive athleticism, with group standings taking shape across both tournaments.

Evening sessions at Expo Park bring cooler air and vibrant crowd energy. The stadium's soccer pedigree translates perfectly to flag football's pitch-sized field, creating an intimate atmosphere for this brand-new event.
FFB02.potentialContendersIntro: USA men and Mexico women are expected to headline this session, with Canada and Japan likely in the women's mix.

FFB03.blurb:
Day two of preliminaries continues Sunday afternoon with six more group-stage contests. Teams carry momentum from opening matches as the tournament landscape begins to clarify, with medal favorites looking to assert dominance.

Japan and Great Britain could clash in women's action, while USA men aim to build on their projected strength. The non-contact format keeps stars healthy while delivering highlight-reel plays as defenders pursue flag pulls instead of tackles.

Sunday afternoons at Exposition Park offer family-friendly timing and accessible pricing. The stadium's 22,000-seat capacity creates buzz without the overwhelming scale of larger venues, perfect for catching this emerging sport.
FFB03.potentialContendersIntro: Mexico leads the women's field per IFAF rankings, with Great Britain and Japan expected to compete. USA men are anticipated as group-stage dominators.

FFB04.blurb:
Sunday night closes the weekend with critical preliminary matchups as teams jockey for semifinal seeding. Six games across men's and women's tournaments could determine which squads advance with momentum versus which face must-win scenarios ahead.

Canada's women and USA's men likely take the field in pivotal contests. Every flag pull and red zone stand matters as point differentials may break ties in the tight 6-team format.

The downtown skyline glows beyond the stadium as evening sets in. Exposition Park's central LA location means easy access from across the metro area, with the buzz of a new sport drawing curious crowds alongside die-hard fans.
FFB04.potentialContendersIntro: Mexico women are favorites per IFAF standings, with Canada also among top contenders. USA men expected to perform strongly throughout group play.

FFB05.blurb:
Monday afternoon marks the midpoint of preliminaries with six more games shaping the bracket. Teams face mounting pressure as the field narrows toward Thursday's semifinals, making every possession crucial in the 20-minute halves.

Japan and Great Britain could deliver a women's showcase, while men's squads battle to avoid elimination scenarios. The 70-yard field compresses defenses, rewarding precision passing and elusiveness in tight spaces.

Afternoon light floods the open-air bowl at Expo Park. The venue's proximity to the California Science Center and Natural History Museum makes it easy to combine flag football with other LA attractions on a Monday visit.
FFB05.potentialContendersIntro: IFAF ranks Mexico No. 1 for women entering the Games, with Japan and Great Britain anticipated as competitors. USA projected as a men's powerhouse.

FFB06.blurb:
Monday evening's session could feature elimination-deciding contests as preliminary rounds near their conclusion. Teams on the bubble face do-or-die scenarios, while top seeds look to lock in favorable semifinal matchups.

Canada's women and USA's men could cement their spots or face upset scares. The non-contact format keeps intensity high without injury risk, but defensive specialists hunt for crucial flag pulls that swing games.

Exposition Park Stadium comes alive under lights with LA's soccer culture bringing vocal support. The venue hosted major international matches before the Games, and flag football inherits that competitive atmosphere as the crowd roars for big plays.
FFB06.potentialContendersIntro: Women's competition led by top-ranked Mexico in IFAF standings, with Canada expected to challenge. USA men favored based on international form.

Tuesday afternoon delivers the penultimate preliminary session with semifinal spots potentially on the line. Six games across both tournaments determine final bracket positioning as group play winds down.

Great Britain, Japan, and Canada could battle in a women's elimination scenario, while USA men aim to finish atop their group. Point differentials may decide tiebreakers, turning every score into a high-stakes moment.

The open-air setting and moderate capacity keep the experience intimate compared to mega-venues. Exposition Park's central location means easy Metro access from downtown LA or Santa Monica, ideal for catching an afternoon session before exploring the city.
FFB07.potentialContendersIntro: Mexico tops women's world rankings per IFAF, with Great Britain, Japan, and Canada noted as contenders. USA projected as top men's team.

The final preliminary session Tuesday night closes group play across both tournaments. Semifinal matchups lock in as teams learn their medal-round opponents, with upsets still possible in winner-take-all scenarios.

Japan's women and USA's men could headline the evening card as favorites secure their spots. The 20-minute halves accelerate drama, with late-game heroics deciding who advances and who goes home.

Expo Park under lights delivers electric atmosphere for the preliminary finale. The venue's history with Angel City FC and LAFC means LA crowds know how to bring energy for high-stakes matches, and flag football gets the full treatment as the sport's debut nears its knockout phase.
FFB08.potentialContendersIntro: Mexico is the No. 1 ranked women's nation per IFAF, with Japan among projected strong teams. USA men expected to dominate early rounds.

FFB09.blurb:
Wednesday afternoon brings additional preliminary action as the tournament format continues. Teams refine execution ahead of Thursday's semifinals, with every snap building toward medal-round intensity.

Canada and Great Britain could feature in women's matchups, while men's squads finalize their approaches. The compact 70-yard field rewards precision and athleticism, creating highlight moments in rapid succession.

Midweek sessions offer excellent value for fans exploring multiple sports. Expo Park's walkable campus includes the Coliseum and museum complex, making it easy to build a full day around an afternoon of flag football.
FFB09.potentialContendersIntro: IFAF ranks Mexico No. 1 in women's flag football, with Canada and Great Britain expected in the mix. USA positioned as men's favorite.

The absolute final preliminary session Wednesday night completes group play before Thursday's semifinals. Six games wrap the opening phase as brackets finalize and medal dreams either advance or end.

Japan's women and USA's men could close preliminaries with statement performances. Teams that survived the gauntlet shift focus to knockout rounds, where single elimination raises the stakes exponentially.

Wednesday night at Expo Park offers one last chance to catch the accessible pricing of preliminaries. The venue's sightlines keep every fan close to the action, with the downtown skyline providing a quintessential LA backdrop as flag football's debut transitions to medal rounds.

The first medals of flag football's historic debut are one day away as the men's semifinals determine who fights for gold. Two games in just over two hours decide which nations advance to Friday's bronze and gold medal matches.

USA men are expected to feature based on prior international dominance, but single elimination means no margin for error. The 70-yard field compresses as defensive schemes tighten and quarterbacks face relentless pressure hunting flags.

Thursday afternoon at Expo Park brings medal-round intensity to the intimate 22,000-seat venue. The stakes escalate dramatically from preliminaries as every play could end a nation's medal hopes in flag football's debut tournament.

Expo Park's open-air atmosphere and downtown views set the stage for history. These semifinals determine who competes for flag football's first women's medals Saturday afternoon, making every possession a defining moment in the sport's debut.

The bronze match opens the session before the gold medal game crowns the sport's inaugural champion. Every flag pull carries historic weight as nations battle for podium spots in front of an LA crowd witnessing the birth of a new tradition.

GAR01.blurb:
Artistic gymnastics opens with the first men's qualification session, where teams and individuals launch their medal campaigns across all six apparatus. Rotations proceed through floor, pommel horse, rings, vault, parallel bars, and horizontal bar, with scores feeding into team, all-around, and apparatus final qualification.

Japan enters as the reigning team champion from Paris 2024, while China brings a deep roster that claimed silver and multiple individual medals. The United States bronze medalists and Philippines standout Carlos Yulo, who swept floor and vault gold in Paris, could appear depending on final rosters.

DTLA Arena's 20,000-seat bowl wraps around the competition floor, offering clear views of every landing and handstand from the upper deck. The morning session draws local families and serious fans who understand the stakes of qualification scoring.
GAR01.potentialContendersIntro: Japan's Paris 2024 team gold and individual all-around champion Shinnosuke Oka could anchor the reigning champions if he competes. China's Zhang Boheng and Xiao Ruoteng, who claimed all-around silver and bronze, are projected contenders.

China's deep apparatus specialists, including Paris 2024 parallel bars champion Zou Jingyuan and rings gold medalist Liu Yang, could feature in this rotation. The United States team bronze contenders and Ireland's Rhys McClenaghan, who won pommel horse gold in Paris, are likely entries if rosters hold.

Afternoon light filters through DTLA Arena's windows, creating a different atmosphere from the morning session. Crowds grow louder as weekend spectators fill the seats, drawn by the circular sightlines that keep every apparatus visible.

GAR04.blurb:
Women's qualification begins with two back-to-back sessions spanning nearly four hours of competition. Teams and individuals rotate through vault, uneven bars, balance beam, and floor exercise, establishing the field for team finals, all-around, and apparatus events.

The United States enters as reigning team champion with Paris 2024 all-around gold medalist Simone Biles and bronze medalist Suni Lee projected to return. Brazil's Rebeca Andrade, who won Paris all-around silver and floor gold, recovered from a 2024 ACL injury and could challenge if she competes. Italy's silver medal team brings Paris balance beam champion Alice D'Amato.

Sunday morning at DTLA Arena draws capacity crowds for the women's debut, with families claiming seats for the extended session. The arena's circular design keeps all four apparatus in view as rotations shift every ninety seconds.
GAR04.potentialContendersIntro: USA's Simone Biles (Paris 2024 all-around and vault gold) and Suni Lee (all-around bronze) are projected to compete if they maintain form. Brazil's Rebeca Andrade (all-around silver, floor gold) returned from ACL injury in 2025 and could contend.

GAR05.blurb:
Qualification continues with session three, a tighter afternoon window featuring additional rotation groups. The compressed schedule keeps the pace brisk as gymnasts cycle through apparatus with minimal downtime.

Italy's Paris silver team, including balance beam medalists Alice D'Amato and Manila Esposito, could feature here. Algeria's Kaylia Nemour, who won uneven bars gold in Paris, represents African gymnastics at the highest level. China's bar specialists and beam contenders round out the projected field.

The mid-afternoon slot at DTLA Arena fills with spectators taking advantage of the shorter session length. Sight lines remain excellent from every angle, and the central scoreboard tracks live standings as qualification totals accumulate.
GAR05.potentialContendersIntro: Italy's Alice D'Amato and Manila Esposito, who won gold and bronze on balance beam in Paris, are expected to compete for the silver team. Algeria's Kaylia Nemour (Paris 2024 uneven bars gold) could bring African representation.

GAR06.blurb:
Session four brings another evening rotation, narrowing the qualification field as scores solidify. Teams on the medal bubble face pressure to post clean routines across all apparatus, while individual specialists target apparatus final berths.

Brazil's bronze team from Paris, anchored by Rebeca Andrade, aims to advance. The United States depth chart could include Paris vault bronze medalist Jade Carey if she qualifies. China's beam and bars specialists Zhou Yaqin and Qiu Qiyuan are projected contenders for apparatus finals.

Early evening at DTLA Arena sees growing crowds as the workday ends and locals arrive. The venue's downtown location makes it accessible for after-work spectators, filling seats quickly for the shorter session.

GAR07.blurb:
The final women's qualification session wraps the weekend under late-night arena lights. Remaining teams and individuals complete their apparatus rotations, locking in the finalists for Tuesday's team showdown and later all-around and apparatus medals.

This session could feature additional depth chart gymnasts from the Paris medalist nations—USA, Italy, and Brazil—plus specialists targeting bars, beam, vault, or floor finals. Romania's Ana Bărbosu, who claimed floor bronze in Paris, represents Eastern European contenders if she qualifies.

DTLA Arena at night becomes an enclosed theater, with spotlights isolating each gymnast against the darkened upper bowl. The 9:25 PM start attracts dedicated fans willing to stay late, creating an intimate atmosphere despite the 20,000-seat capacity.

GAR08.blurb:
The men's team final delivers the first gymnastics medal session, with eight nations competing for gold. Five gymnasts per team, four compete on each apparatus, three scores count—every routine carries weight, and a single fall can cost the podium.

Japan defends its Paris 2024 gold with a projected lineup including Shinnosuke Oka. China's silver team brings apparatus champions Zou Jingyuan and Liu Yang. The United States bronze squad could feature pommel horse specialist Stephen Nedoroscik if rosters hold.

DTLA Arena transforms into a cauldron for finals, with 20,000 spectators creating the noise level typically reserved for playoff basketball. The circular bowl amplifies crowd reactions as rotations shift and medal hopes rise or fall with each landing.
GAR08.potentialContendersIntro: Japan's Paris 2024 gold team, likely featuring Shinnosuke Oka (all-around and horizontal bar champion), defends its title. China's silver team brings Zou Jingyuan (parallel bars gold) and Liu Yang (rings gold). USA's bronze squad could include Stephen Nedoroscik (pommel horse bronze).

The women's team final crowns the second gymnastics champion, with eight squads battling across vault, bars, beam, and floor. The same scoring format applies—four up, three count—turning every wobble or stuck landing into a tactical calculation.

The United States defends Paris 2024 gold with a projected roster including Simone Biles. Italy's silver team, featuring balance beam champion Alice D'Amato, aims to close the gap. Brazil's bronze squad, led by Rebeca Andrade if she competes, brings floor and vault firepower.

Tuesday evening at DTLA Arena sees the venue at peak capacity, with the central scoreboard tracking team totals in real time. The circular design ensures no bad seats—every rotation is visible, every cheer echoes across the bowl.

GAR10.blurb:
The men's all-around final determines who can master all six apparatus in a single session. Twenty-four gymnasts qualified through prelims compete for the title, rotating through floor, pommel horse, rings, vault, parallel bars, and horizontal bar over two and a half hours.

Japan's Shinnosuke Oka defends his Paris 2024 gold if he competes. China's Zhang Boheng and Xiao Ruoteng, who won silver and bronze in Paris, are projected contenders. The all-around demands versatility—a pommel horse disaster can erase a brilliant floor routine.

Wednesday evening crowds at DTLA Arena understand the stakes of all-around competition, tracking cumulative scores on the central board as gymnasts move through rotations. The circular venue keeps every apparatus in view, allowing spectators to follow the medal race in real time.
GAR10.potentialContendersIntro: Japan's Shinnosuke Oka defends his Paris 2024 all-around gold. China's Zhang Boheng (silver) and Xiao Ruoteng (bronze) are expected contenders if rosters hold. The field features the most versatile gymnasts across all six apparatus.

GAR11.blurb:
The women's all-around final showcases 24 gymnasts competing across vault, bars, beam, and floor for the most prestigious individual title. Consistency matters—beam wobbles and floor landings separate podium finishers from the rest of the field.

Simone Biles defends her Paris 2024 gold if she competes, maintaining top world rankings post-Paris. Rebeca Andrade, who won silver in Paris and returned strong from ACL injury in 2025, could challenge. Suni Lee, bronze medalist in Paris, placed fourth at the 2025 World Championships after recovering from kidney issues.

Thursday night at DTLA Arena becomes a showcase event, drawing celebrity spectators and peak ticket prices. The venue's sight lines and central scoreboard create theater-in-the-round drama as rotation scores accumulate toward the podium.
GAR11.potentialContendersIntro: USA's Simone Biles defends her Paris 2024 all-around gold, having maintained top rankings at recent World Championships. Brazil's Rebeca Andrade (silver) returned from ACL injury with strong 2025 performances. USA's Suni Lee (bronze) placed fourth at 2025 Worlds after kidney recovery.

GAR12.blurb:
Apparatus finals begin with the first session awarding medals across multiple events. Eight finalists per apparatus compete in rotations, with the highest single score determining the podium—no room for error, no second chances.

This session likely features men's horizontal bar (Shinnosuke Oka won gold in Paris) and women's balance beam (Alice D'Amato took gold). Floor exercise, pommel horse, and vault could also appear depending on FIG scheduling. Specialists who trained for years on a single apparatus chase their medal moment.

Saturday morning at DTLA Arena draws serious gymnastics fans who understand apparatus nuance—the difference between a stuck landing and a hop, a clean handstand and a slight shift. The venue's acoustics amplify gasps when routines wobble and roars when they stick.
GAR12.potentialContendersIntro: Projected events include men's horizontal bar (Shinnosuke Oka won Paris gold) and women's balance beam (Alice D'Amato gold, Zhou Yaqin silver, Manila Esposito bronze from Paris). Exact apparatus lineup awaits FIG scheduling.

GAR13.blurb:
The second apparatus finals session continues medal distribution across remaining events. Vault, floor exercise, rings, pommel horse, uneven bars—whichever events weren't contested Saturday now take center stage.

This session could feature men's floor (Carlos Yulo won Paris gold) and women's uneven bars (Kaylia Nemour took gold). Pommel horse (Rhys McClenaghan won Paris gold) or men's rings (Liu Yang claimed gold) are also possible. Each apparatus final runs tight—eight competitors, best score wins.

Sunday late morning at DTLA Arena maintains the apparatus finals atmosphere, with crowds that skew toward knowledgeable fans. The circular venue design keeps the action intimate despite the 20,000 capacity, with spectators close enough to hear chalk slaps and landing thuds.
GAR13.potentialContendersIntro: Projected events include men's floor (Carlos Yulo won Paris gold) and women's uneven bars (Kaylia Nemour gold, Qiu Qiyuan silver, Suni Lee bronze from Paris). Men's pommel horse (Rhys McClenaghan gold) or rings (Liu Yang gold) could also feature.

GAR14.blurb:
The final apparatus session completes gymnastics medal events, awarding the last individual titles. Parallel bars, vault, floor, or whichever events remain after Saturday and Sunday now crown their champions in quick succession.

This session could include men's parallel bars (Zou Jingyuan won Paris gold) and men's vault (Carlos Yulo claimed gold). Women's floor exercise (Rebeca Andrade took Paris gold) or women's vault (Simone Biles won gold) are also possible depending on schedule. The longest session of apparatus finals—two and a half hours—suggests multiple events.

Monday late morning crowds at DTLA Arena see gymnastics conclude after ten days of competition. The venue's downtown location makes it accessible for local fans taking late morning off work, filling seats for the sport's final medals.
GAR14.potentialContendersIntro: Projected events include men's parallel bars (Zou Jingyuan Paris gold), men's vault (Carlos Yulo gold), women's floor (Rebeca Andrade gold, Simone Biles silver from Paris), or women's vault (Biles gold, Andrade silver, Jade Carey bronze). Exact lineup awaits FIG scheduling.

GAR15.blurb:
The mixed team final introduces a new gymnastics format pending FIG approval, pairing men's and women's disciplines in a combined team competition. Exact structure—apparatus rotations, scoring, team composition—awaits final rule confirmation.

No Paris 2024 precedent exists, making this a genuinely novel event. Projected contenders include nations with depth across both programs: USA (gold in women's team, bronze in men's), China (silver in both), Japan (men's gold), Italy (women's silver), and Brazil (women's bronze).

Tuesday evening at DTLA Arena sees the sport close with an experimental format, drawing curious crowds eager to witness the first-ever mixed team medals. The venue's circular design and central scoreboard adapt easily to the new event structure, whatever it proves to be.
GAR15.potentialContendersIntro: Format and rules pending FIG approval. Nations with strength across both programs—USA (Paris 2024 women's gold, men's bronze), China (silver in both), Japan (men's gold), Italy (women's silver), Brazil (women's bronze)—are projected contenders if the event proceeds.

GLF01.blurb:
The men's competition opens on Riviera's storied fairways, where the world's top-ranked male golfers begin their 72-hole quest for gold. Each player navigates George C. Thomas Jr.'s strategic Golden Age design—elevation swings, barranca hazards, and approach shots shaped by Pacific breezes.

Qualification draws primarily from the Official World Golf Ranking's top 15, capping four per nation, so expect reigning world-class talent teeing off in morning and afternoon waves. Paris 2024 gold medalist Scottie Scheffler could return if he qualifies, alongside silver medalist Tommy Fleetwood and bronze medalist Hideki Matsuyama.

Spectators roam the walkable canyon layout, following featured groups through eucalyptus groves and kikuyu rough. Grandstands ring signature holes; early-round energy builds as leaderboards shift and medal contenders emerge over the first 18.
GLF01.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024's podium finishers lead the projected field if they qualify, joined by other top-ranked pros likely to represent their nations.

GLF02.blurb:
Round two separates contenders from the pack as the cut line looms and leaderboard pressure mounts. Players attack Riviera's risk-reward par-5s and defend against its treacherous short par-4s, where a single misjudged wedge can erase a morning's gains.

Expect Paris medalists Scheffler, Fleetwood, and Matsuyama to feature prominently if they qualified, though the 60-player field will include rising stars and major champions from every continent. With a four-per-country cap, national depth matters—some nations field multiple world-top-20 golfers.

Galleries thicken around moving-day storylines by late afternoon. The course's open sightlines let fans track multiple groups simultaneously; roars echo across the canyon when someone makes a charge or stumbles at the iconic 10th green.
GLF02.potentialContendersIntro: The likely field again features Paris 2024's top three finishers, assuming qualification through world rankings.

GLF03.blurb:
Moving day at Riviera transforms the tournament into a medal race. Leaders protect narrow advantages while chasers gamble on aggressive lines—Riviera's barranca left of the 18th fairway has swallowed many comeback bids, but its reachable par-5 6th offers eagle chances to vault up the board.

Paris 2024 champions Scheffler, Fleetwood, and Matsuyama could be in the hunt if they qualified and found form through two rounds. The weekend cut ensures only the top performers advance, sharpening the drama as national pride hangs on every putt.

Fans pack hospitality areas overlooking the course's dramatic elevation changes, watching approach shots to elevated greens and downhill tee shots framed by the Santa Monica Mountains. Third-round roars preview Sunday's climax.
GLF03.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024's medalists remain central to the projected narrative if they made the cut and stay in contention.

GLF04.blurb:
Gold, silver, and bronze are decided over 18 final holes on one of golf's most celebrated championship tests. Riviera's closing stretch—par-3 14th over water, drivable par-4 10th, cape-style 18th with its iconic kikuyu bunker—has crowned major champions and will now anoint 2028 medalists.

Defending gold medalist Scottie Scheffler could be chasing history if he qualified and contended through 54 holes. Paris silver medalist Tommy Fleetwood and bronze medalist Hideki Matsuyama are also projected threats, but any top-15 world-ranked player in the field can seize the moment on a single hot round.

Galleries follow final groups through the Pacific Palisades canyon, where ocean breezes shift club selections and every birdie roar reverberates. The 18th green sits below the historic clubhouse—medals are won or lost on that sloping putting surface as crowds press close.
GLF04.potentialContendersIntro: The likely medal chase includes Paris 2024's podium finishers if they qualified, though the 72-hole format ensures surprises.

GLF05.blurb:
The mixed team event debuts at the 2028 Games, pairing one man and one woman already qualified for individual stroke play into 16 national duos. This preliminary round uses four-ball best-ball format—both players hit each hole, the better score counts—rewarding aggressive play and partner chemistry over 18 holes at Riviera.

Nations can field only one mixed team, so expect powerhouse pairings: dominant major champions teaming with rising stars, veterans mentoring younger talents, or strategic combinations built for match-play dynamics. Countries like the United States, Great Britain, Japan, and South Korea could assemble formidable duos if their ranked players opt in.

Fans track multiple matches simultaneously across the course, catching birdie barrages on the par-5s and clutch par saves on Riviera's devilish short holes. The format is new; the stakes—advancing with momentum into Monday's foursomes final—are immediate.
GLF05.potentialContendersIntro: Projected mixed teams will pair top male and female qualifiers from nations with depth in both rankings, though rosters won't be known until closer to the event.

GLF06.blurb:
Mixed team medals are decided in alternate-shot foursomes, where partners hit the same ball in sequence—one drives, the other approaches, and so on for 18 holes of tactical chess. Riviera's narrow fairways and strategic hazards punish miscommunication; successful teams balance risk and execution under pressure.

The 16 pairs carry their four-ball results into this final round, combining 36-hole totals to determine gold, silver, and bronze. Expect the field to feature duos from the United States, Japan, Great Britain, South Korea, and other nations that qualified strong individual competitors and chose to enter the team event.

Spectators witness a format unseen in the Games since 1904, watching national partnerships navigate Riviera's kikuyu rough and elevated greens together. Cheers erupt when a clutch putt salvages par or a partner's recovery shot saves a hole—team golf at its most collaborative.
GLF06.potentialContendersIntro: Medal contenders will include pairings from nations with top-ranked male and female qualifiers, though specific rosters remain speculative until official entries are announced.

GLF07.blurb:
The women's individual competition opens after the mixed team concludes, bringing the world's top female golfers to Riviera for their own 72-hole stroke play battle. Round one sets the tone—navigating barranca carries, reading kikuyu grain on undulating greens, and managing Pacific breezes that gust through the canyon.

Qualification mirrors the men's: top 15 in the Women's World Golf Rankings with a four-per-country cap, ensuring major champions and tour stars from every continent. Expect reigning world number-ones, recent major winners, and breakout talents vying for positioning over the opening 18 holes.

Fans explore Riviera's walkable Golden Age layout, following featured groups through eucalyptus shade and open fairways. Early leaderboards are fluid; birdies at the reachable par-5s can vault unknowns into contention, while Riviera's short, treacherous par-4s punish overconfidence.
GLF07.potentialContendersIntro: The projected women's field will include the world's top-ranked players based on qualification through the Women's World Golf Rankings, with major champions and rising stars competing for medals.

GLF08.blurb:
Round two sharpens the women's medal race as players jockey for weekend position. Riviera's risk-reward holes—drivable par-4 10th, reachable par-5 17th—tempt aggressive play, but the course punishes mistakes with thick kikuyu rough and sloping greens that reject poorly struck approaches.

The 60-player field likely features multiple major champions, world number-one contenders, and athletes from nations with deep women's golf traditions like South Korea, the United States, Japan, and Thailand. With four players per country allowed, some nations will have internal medal battles within the larger competition.

Galleries thicken as Friday approaches and cut-line drama builds. The open layout lets spectators track several groups, catching the roar when someone holes out from the fairway or drains a long birdie putt. Leaderboards shift hourly; momentum matters.
GLF08.potentialContendersIntro: Top-ranked players from the women's world rankings are projected to lead the field, though specific names depend on 2028 qualification results.

GLF09.blurb:
Moving day for the women separates medal contenders from the field. Leaders defend against charges; chasers attack Riviera's scoring holes with calculated aggression. The barranca left of 18 looms over every final approach—playing safe might cost a medal, but one push could end the chase entirely.

Expect the leaderboard to feature a mix of established stars—past major winners, world number-one candidates—and emerging talents who've thrived on Riviera's strategic test through two rounds. National pride intensifies as players represent their countries in a global showcase.

Fans pack sightlines around pivotal holes, watching approach shots to Riviera's elevated greens and downhill tee balls framed by canyon walls. Third-round roars preview Sunday's climax; the course's walkability lets crowds follow their favorites across all 18 holes.
GLF09.potentialContendersIntro: The likely medal chase includes top qualifiers from the Women's World Golf Rankings, with major champions and tour stars expected to contend if they've made the cut.

GLF10.blurb:
Gold, silver, and bronze are decided over 18 final holes on Riviera's championship layout. The closing stretch—par-3 14th over water, strategic par-4 15th, cape-style 18th with its iconic bunker—has tested legends since 1926 and will now crown the women's 2028 medalists.

The lowest 72-hole total claims gold; a single stroke separates glory from heartbreak. Expect world-class competitors from nations with rich women's golf histories—South Korea's tour dominance, America's depth, Japan's rising generation, Europe's major champions—to battle down the stretch.

Crowds press close to the 18th green below the historic clubhouse, where every final putt carries the weight of four years' preparation. Ocean breezes complicate club selection; Riviera's kikuyu grain challenges even the steadiest stroke. Medals are won or lost on that sloping surface as the canyon echoes with cheers.
GLF10.potentialContendersIntro: The projected final-round leaderboard will feature top qualifiers from the Women's World Golf Rankings, with major champions and world number-one contenders likely in the medal hunt if they've contended through 54 holes.

GRY01.blurb:
The first wave of rhythmic gymnastics qualification brings the world's top individual competitors to Exposition Park, each performing four apparatus routines—hoop, ball, clubs, and ribbon—to secure a place in Friday's final. Expect grace meets athleticism as gymnasts blend balletic elegance with acrobatic precision, their apparatus flying through intricate patterns while judges assess difficulty, execution, and artistry.

This afternoon session offers an accessible entry point to witness Paris 2024 medalists and rising contenders building their scores across all four disciplines. The top performers from both qualification sessions advance to compete for medals.

The Galen Center's intimate arena configuration seats you close to the floor, where you'll see the shimmer of costumes, hear the music's every beat, and feel the tension of apparatus catches that must land perfectly.
GRY01.potentialContendersIntro: The field is expected to include Paris 2024 medalists and world championship contenders from the European and Asian powers that have dominated recent competitions.

GRY02.blurb:
The second qualification session completes the individual field, with the remaining competitors performing all four apparatus routines under the Galen Center lights. Ribbon routines unfurl in sweeping spirals, hoops trace geometric arcs, and club juggling sequences test the limits of hand-eye coordination—all set to music that ranges from classical to contemporary.

Scores from both afternoon and evening sessions combine to determine the ten finalists who will compete for medals Friday night. Watch for newcomers challenging established stars and national rivalries playing out through tenths of points.

The evening atmosphere brings heightened energy as gymnasts deliver their best work, knowing these three-plus hours determine whether their Games end here or continue to the medal round.
GRY02.potentialContendersIntro: Additional contenders likely include gymnasts from traditional powerhouses Bulgaria, Israel, and Ukraine, alongside emerging talents from Asia and Eastern Europe.

GRY03.blurb:
Five gymnasts moving as one—group rhythmic gymnastics brings synchronized choreography and dramatic apparatus exchanges to the competition floor. Teams perform two routines featuring hoops, ribbons, balls, or clubs, their movements timed to split-second precision as apparatus crisscross the floor in coordinated patterns.

The qualifying round determines which eight groups advance to Saturday's final, with scores based on technical difficulty, synchronization quality, and artistic composition. China enters as defending champion, while European squads Israel and Italy return as Paris 2024 medalists.

The Galen Center's sightlines let you track all five athletes simultaneously, catching the seamless handoffs and formation changes that separate elite groups from the rest of the field.
GRY03.potentialContendersIntro: The Paris 2024 podium nations are expected to qualify, alongside traditional group powers from Eastern Europe and Asia.

potentialContenders.note: Perennial group contender, likely to qualify

GRY04.blurb:
The individual all-around final crowns rhythmic gymnastics' ultimate champion—the gymnast who masters all four apparatus with the most complete blend of technical mastery and artistic vision. Ten qualifiers return to perform hoop, ball, clubs, and ribbon one final time, their cumulative scores determining gold, silver, and bronze.

Germany's Darja Varfolomeev arrives as the Paris 2024 gold medalist and the athlete to beat, while Bulgaria's Boryana Kaleyn and Italy's Sofia Raffaeli could challenge if they qualify. Every toss, every rotation, every musical accent matters—medals often separate by fractions of a point.

The Friday evening finals session brings peak atmosphere to the Galen Center, with spotlights illuminating each routine and the crowd hushed during performances, erupting after final poses. This is where four years of training culminate in eleven minutes of competition.
GRY04.potentialContendersIntro: The Paris 2024 podium is expected to feature prominently if those athletes qualify, though world rankings and 2025-2028 competition results will shape the final field.

potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold medalist, the gymnast to beat if she qualifies

GRY05.blurb:
Eight synchronized teams compete for the group all-around medals in rhythmic gymnastics' team spectacular. Each squad performs two routines, delivering choreographed sequences where five athletes mirror each other's movements while exchanging apparatus mid-flight—ribbons streaming, hoops rotating, formations shifting in balletic unison.

China defends its Paris 2024 title against European challengers Israel and Italy, both podium finishers four years prior. Group routines emphasize teamwork and theatrical flair, with dramatic tosses and catches that must be executed flawlessly by all five members simultaneously.

The Saturday afternoon final offers a shorter, high-intensity session where the Galen Center floor becomes a stage for precision choreography. You'll witness the culmination of thousands of hours spent achieving the split-second timing that separates medal contenders from the rest.
GRY05.potentialContendersIntro: The group final is expected to showcase the Paris 2024 medalist nations alongside traditional Eastern European powers, if they qualify through Friday's preliminary round.

GTR01.blurb:
The men's trampoline competition delivers everything in one session—qualification and final unfold the same afternoon at DTLA Arena. Eight athletes advance from the opening round straight to the medal chase, where gravity-defying routines combine technical precision with high-flying spectacle.

Paris 2024 gold medalist Ivan Litvinovich leads a field expected to feature China's dominant squad alongside rising contenders from Europe and North America. Each routine stacks twists, flips, and somersaults in rapid succession—judges score both difficulty and execution, rewarding athletes who stick complex combinations without sacrificing form.

DTLA Arena's circular design puts every seat close to the action, and the central scoreboard captures slow-motion replays of athletes reaching heights up to 26 feet above the trampoline bed. The crowd builds from the controlled intensity of qualification to the electric final rounds where medals hang on tenths of a point.
GTR01.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 medalists Ivan Litvinovich (gold), Wang Zisai (silver), and Yan Langyu (bronze) are projected to return if they qualify through the 2027 World Championships pathway.

potentialContenders.name: Ivan Litvinovich

GTR02.blurb:
Friday night trampoline brings the women's competition from first bounce to final medal. Qualification narrows a global field to eight finalists, then the arena goes dark for introductions and the high-stakes final where careers can pivot on a single routine.

Bryony Page claimed Britain's first trampoline gold in Paris, breaking through against strong challenges from Eastern Europe and Canada. Expect technical innovation—athletes push difficulty scores higher each cycle while fighting to maintain the flawless form judges demand.

The evening session transforms DTLA Arena into a theater of precision and risk. Spotlights track athletes as they soar, and the circular seating wraps the crowd around the trampoline bed. Between routines, scoreboards flash rankings that shift with every landing, holding 20,000 spectators in suspense until the final rotation.
GTR02.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 champion Bryony Page could defend her title if she qualifies, alongside medalists Viyaleta Bardzilouskaya (silver) and Sophiane Méthot (bronze).

HBL01.blurb:
The 2028 Summer Games handball competition opens with two men's preliminary matches as twelve nations begin their medal chase. Defending champion Denmark, along with Paris 2024 finalists Germany and bronze medalist Spain, are projected to headline a deep field.

Each match unfolds as a 60-minute sprint of fast breaks, physical defense, and strategic goalkeeper substitutions. Teams play five preliminary games to determine quarterfinal seeding, making every goal valuable.

Long Beach Arena's intimate 14,000-seat setting creates acoustic intensity — expect thunderous horns from European supporter sections and the rhythmic thud of the ball hitting hardwood echoing off the 77-foot ceiling.
HBL01.potentialContendersIntro: Denmark enters as the Paris 2024 gold medalist, while Germany and Spain could return as top contenders based on their 2024 performances.

Expect European powerhouses to control tempo against emerging programs, but upsets happen — handball's 6-on-7 attacking strategies and two-minute power plays create volatile momentum swings.

Afternoon light filters through Long Beach Arena's upper concourse, but inside the venue feels intimate and loud, with every shot block and turnover amplified by the crowd pressed close to the court.
HBL02.potentialContendersIntro: Germany, the Paris 2024 silver medalist, could appear alongside Norway, Croatia, and other established European programs.

Preliminary round is where reputations shift: a Paris 2024 bronze medalist can stumble against a surging qualifier, and goal differential matters as much as wins. These matches set the tone for two weeks of competition.

Under the lights, Long Beach Arena transforms — the scoreboard glows, the court shines, and the crowd energy builds through both matches, creating the kind of atmosphere this venue delivered for 1984 Games volleyball.
HBL03.potentialContendersIntro: Spain, the Paris 2024 bronze medalist, is expected to compete, along with Sweden and Slovenia from the Paris Group A field.

Opening day of men's field hockey delivers two pool matches kicking off the tournament at Carson Field. Each game carries weight in the round-robin standings—three points for a win, goal differential matters, and every result shapes quarterfinal seeding.

JUD01.blurb:
The lightest weight classes open judo competition as the women's -48kg and men's -60kg divisions begin their elimination rounds. Athletes compete in single-elimination brackets with repechage opportunities for those who lose to eventual finalists, setting the stage for afternoon medal bouts.

Each nation fields one competitor per weight class, chosen from the top 17 IJF world rankings built over two years of qualifying events. Expect explosive technique from smaller athletes whose speed and precision can end matches in seconds with ippon-scoring throws.

LA Convention Center Hall 2 transforms its exhibit space into an intimate combat arena where you'll hear every judogi grip adjustment and feel the crowd react to near-throws. Morning prelims offer accessible entry to see world-class judo up close before the evening finals crowd arrives.
